title: Signed Numbers and Two's Complement
summary: Represent negative numbers in binary using sign-magnitude, one's complement, and two's complement.
content_html:
To represent negative numbers in binary, computers use two's complement as the standard method.

Sign-Magnitude (Not Used in Practice)

Use the MSB (leftmost bit) as a sign bit: 0 = positive, 1 = negative. Problem: two representations of zero (+0 and -0) and complex arithmetic.

Two's Complement (Standard)

For an n-bit number:

  • Positive numbers: same as unsigned binary
  • Negative numbers: flip all bits then add 1
Example: -5 in 8-bit two's complement
+5  = 00000101
Flip = 11111010
Add 1 = 11111011  ← this is -5

The range for n-bit two's complement is −2^(n-1) to 2^(n-1) − 1.

8-bit: −128 to +127
16-bit: −32,768 to +32,767
32-bit: −2,147,483,648 to +2,147,483,647

Detecting Negative in Two's Complement

If the most significant bit (MSB) is 1, the number is negative.

Benefits of Two's Complement

  • Only one representation of zero
  • Addition and subtraction use the same hardware circuit
  • Subtraction: A − B = A + (−B) = A + two's_complement(B)
